If a clot travels to the brain it causes a stroke — which is why people with AFib are often prescribed blood-thinning medication.

instruction: Simplify this medical term for someone with no medical knowledge
output: Your bones have a sponge-like structure inside — lots of tiny holes held together by a hard frame. In healthy bones this structure is dense and strong. Osteoporosis means those holes have grown much bigger, making the whole structure fragile and easy to break, sometimes even from a minor stumble or bump. The problem is there are no warning signs — you feel nothing until a bone actually breaks. The most dangerous fractures are the hip, which can be life-altering in older people, and the spine. It is most common in women after the menopause and in older men. Calcium, vitamin D, regular weight-bearing exercise, and certain medications all help to strengthen bones and reduce fracture risk.

instruction: Simplify this medical term for someone with no medical knowledge
output: In type 1 diabetes the immune system has destroyed the cells in the pancreas that make insulin — the hormone that allows your body to use sugar from food for energy. Without any insulin, sugar builds up dangerously in the blood while the body's cells are effectively starving because they cannot absorb it. Unlike type 2 diabetes, this is not caused by lifestyle choices — it is an autoimmune condition that most often appears in children and young adults. People with type 1 diabetes must take insulin every single day for the rest of their lives, usually by injections or an insulin pump, and check their blood sugar levels multiple times daily. Without insulin it is rapidly life-threatening.

instruction: Simplify this medical term for someone with no medical knowledge
output: In rheumatoid arthritis the immune system makes a mistake and starts attacking the lining of your own joints, causing them to become inflamed, painful, swollen, and stiff. Over time, if it is not controlled, this inflammation erodes the cartilage and bone inside the joint, causing lasting damage and deformity. It is very different from the wear-and-tear arthritis most people develop with age — this is an immune system problem that can affect people of any age, even young adults. The most commonly affected joints are the hands, wrists, and feet, usually on both sides of the body at the same time. Modern medicines can suppress the immune attack effectively and many patients achieve full remission.

instruction: Describe this medical condition as if explaining to a patient with no medical background
output: Imagine your nerve fibres as electrical cables. Normally they are coated with a protective insulating layer, called myelin, that makes signals travel quickly and accurately. In multiple sclerosis, your immune system mistakenly attacks and strips away this insulation at multiple points throughout the brain and spinal cord. Where the insulation is damaged, signals slow down, get scrambled, or cannot get through at all. This can cause a very wide range of symptoms depending on which cables are affected — problems with vision, weakness on one side of the body, numbness and tingling, difficulty with balance and coordination, bladder problems, and fatigue that is much more severe than ordinary tiredness. Many people have periods of flare-ups followed by recovery, though some accumulate disability over time.

instruction: Describe this medical condition as if explaining to a patient with no medical background
output: Between your stomach and the tube leading from your mouth to your stomach — the oesophagus — there is a valve that should only open to let food down and then close tightly. In GERD this valve does not work properly and keeps allowing the strong acid from your stomach to wash back up into the oesophagus. Unlike the stomach, which has a special protective lining, the oesophagus is not built to handle acid, so it gets irritated and inflamed. This causes the burning feeling in your chest known as heartburn, the sour taste in your mouth, sometimes a persistent cough or hoarse voice. Long-term uncontrolled acid reflux can permanently damage the oesophageal lining, so it is worth treating properly rather than just putting up with it.

instruction: Describe this medical condition as if explaining to a patient with no medical background
output: Septic shock is the most dangerous stage of sepsis — when a severe infection causes such widespread chaos in the body that blood pressure drops to dangerously low levels that cannot be corrected just by giving fluids. Think of your blood vessels as a network of pipes that normally maintain the right pressure to push blood to every organ. In septic shock, inflammation from the infection causes those pipes to leak and dilate uncontrollably, pressure collapses, and the blood supply to the brain, heart, kidneys, and other vital organs is cut off. Without extremely rapid intervention in intensive care — powerful medications to restore pressure, antibiotics, and oxygen — vital organs begin to fail and death can follow within hours.

instruction: Describe this medical condition as if explaining to a patient with no medical background
output: The brain communicates through tiny electrical signals. In epilepsy, the brain has a tendency to produce sudden bursts of abnormal electrical activity — like an electrical short circuit — that disrupt normal brain function. Each burst is called a seizure. Seizures can look very different depending on which part of the brain is affected. Some cause the dramatic full-body shaking most people associate with the word, but others can simply cause a person to stare blankly for a few seconds, feel a strange sensation, or have an involuntary twitch in one limb. Most people with epilepsy can have their seizures well controlled with daily medication, and many lead entirely normal lives. Some types of epilepsy, particularly in children, are outgrown completely.
